title: Does CLIP’s generalization performance mainly stem from high train-test similarity?

reviews.summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.
reviews.strengths: The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.
reviews.weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.
reviews.questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.

Strengths:
The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.

Weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.

Questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper rigorously investigates the underlying reasons for the exceptional zero-shot generalization performance of foundation models like CLIP, particularly their high out-of-distribution (OOD) accuracy on benchmarks such as ImageNet-Sketch. A common hypothesis attributes this to the vast pre-training datasets, like LAION, containing images perceptually similar to OOD test sets, thus making them not truly "out-of-distribution."

The authors address this crucial and timely question by proposing a novel, systematic interventional approach. They define perceptual similarity using CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings and introduce a "similarity gap" metric, which measures the minimum distance of each image in the test set to images in the training set. They then systematically prune LAION-200M to create subsets where the similarity gap to OOD benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R) matches that of ImageNet-Train.

In their first experiment, iteratively removing images similar to OOD datasets from LAION-200M generally led to a performance decrease, suggesting the importance of these similar images. However, their central and surprising finding is that even after removing these 'highly similar images' through their similarity gap pruning methodology, retrained CLIP models exhibit only a marginal performance drop. They still significantly outperform models trained solely on smaller, in-distribution datasets like ImageNet. This suggests that high train-test similarity alone is insufficient to explain CLIP's robust generalization, pointing instead to other properties like dataset scale and diversity as primary drivers. The work also introduces and releases a 100M core set of LAION that matches the original CLIP performance, a valuable practical contribution for the community.

**Strengths:**
The paper addresses a fundamental and timely question regarding the generalization of large foundation models, challenging a common intuition with a novel and systematic interventional approach. The concept of aligning the 'similarity gap' between datasets to isolate the effect of highly similar images is a significant methodological contribution. The work is technically sound and rigorously executed, with extensive and comprehensive experiments covering various pruning strategies and ImageNet-based OOD benchmarks. The authors are careful in their analysis, clearly defining 'highly similar images' and acknowledging the limitations of their approach. The manuscript is exceptionally well-written, logically structured, and easy to follow, with clear figures and effective discussions. The findings are highly significant for understanding the mechanisms behind the impressive generalization of large vision-language models, redirecting attention to other data properties, and fostering new research avenues. The reproducibility statement and code availability are commendable.

**Weaknesses:**
Despite the extensive experiments, the analysis is primarily focused on variations of ImageNet-based OOD datasets. While an ablation on embedding choice is provided, the reliance on a single type of dataset for the main experiments (variations of ImageNet) limits the generalizability of the conclusions. It is not entirely clear if these analyses hold for other, more diverse OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. This raises questions about the scope of the findings and whether the observed phenomena are specific to ImageNet-like shifts.

Furthermore, some technical details and strong claims could benefit from greater precision and rigorous support. For instance, the assumption that ImageNet-Train is a 'subset' of LAION-400M, while clarified later in the methodology, creates an initial methodological sequencing flaw that could be improved. The claim that 'dissimilar samples... act more like noise' requires more rigorous justification or statistical analysis beyond performance stability. The definition of 'normalized similarity' in Appendix F also appears ambiguous and needs clarification to be technically sound.

Finally, the paper suffers from numerous minor inconsistencies in formatting, terminology, and presentation. Examples include inconsistent capitalization in section titles, varying abbreviations for 'Appendix,' and the presentation of dataset sizes as negative reductions rather than absolute numbers in Table 1. While minor, the cumulative effect of these issues detracts from the overall clarity and meticulousness expected for a high-quality scientific publication. The discussion of the 'effective robustness line' from Fang et al. in Section 6 also lacks sufficient context for readers unfamiliar with that specific detail.

**Questions:**
1.  Could the authors elaborate on the generalizability of their findings to other OOD datasets unrelated to ImageNet, such as iWILDCam or FMoW? The paper mentions 'near-pruning' on MNIST/SVHN sometimes increasing performance; could this observation be expanded upon to suggest if 'highly similar images' are beneficial only for specific types of OOD shifts?
2.  Why was only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings used for filtering? Would larger CLIP models, with potentially better embeddings, provide more accurate filtering of data points and perhaps lead to different conclusions?
3.  The paper concludes that "other properties of LAION-400M must play a role" in driving generalization. While acknowledging this is future work, could the authors elaborate on their hypotheses regarding which specific data properties (e.g., semantic diversity, fine-grained details, specific visual styles, or the quality/compositionality of text captions) might be most impactful, based on their observations? Furthermore, how might one design rigorous experiments to quantitatively disentangle and measure the individual contributions of 'scale' versus 'diversity'?
4.  The 'combined-pruned' split shows a more noticeable drop in performance across all six evaluation sets compared to single-benchmark pruning. Could the authors provide a more detailed analysis of the characteristics of the samples removed in this combined pruning strategy, and discuss the implications of this collective removal versus individual 'highly similar images'?
5.  Regarding the definition of 'highly similar images' relative to ImageNet-Train, how does the choice of ImageNet-Train as the reference dataset (DS) impact the interpretation of the results? Specifically, when ImageNet-Train images are included with standardized captions like "a photo of a {object class}", could this introduce a bias compared to LAION's more diverse and noisy captions?
6.  Please provide a more rigorous justification or statistical analysis to support the strong assertion in Section 4.2 that 'dissimilar samples... act more like noise' and do not contribute to CLIP's performance.
7.  For Table 1, please present the 'Size' column for pruned datasets as the absolute number of samples (e.g., '198.6M') rather than a negative reduction, and either implement the described color coding in the table or remove the descriptive text about colors.
8.  Please ensure consistent capitalization for all section and subsection headers throughout the manuscript and appendices, and clarify the definition of 'normalized similarity' in Appendix F. Could the 'effective robustness line' from Fang et al. (2022) in Section 6 be elaborated upon with a brief explanation or a conceptual plot for better context?

**Rating:** 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.
reviews.questions: What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

Strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.

Questions:
What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper investigates the generalization behavior of CLIP, particularly its exceptional out-of-distribution (OOD) performance, from the perspective of its web-scale training data. The authors propose a novel methodology that uses perceptual similarity in CLIP's embedding space to quantify train-test similarity. They systematically prune LAION-200M to create subsets where the similarity gap to common OOD benchmarks (like ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R) matches that of ImageNet-Train. Surprisingly, models trained on these pruned datasets exhibit only a marginal performance drop, still significantly outperforming ImageNet-trained models. This leads to the conclusion that high train-test similarity alone is insufficient to explain CLIP's impressive OOD generalization, pointing instead to other factors such as dataset scale and diversity. The work also introduces and releases a 100M core set of LAION that is shown to match the original CLIP performance.

Strengths:
1.  **Originality and Timeliness:** The paper addresses a crucial, timely, and fundamental question regarding the generalization mechanisms of large foundation models. The concept of aligning the 'similarity gap' between different training distributions (LAION vs. ImageNet-Train) to systematically prune data is a novel and systematic methodological contribution, providing a powerful interventional tool for studying dataset properties.
2.  **Well-Motivated and Rigorous Experimentation:** The experiments are well motivated, technically sound, and rigorously designed, resulting in compelling and significant results. The authors conduct extensive experiments, including various pruning strategies, diverse ImageNet-based OOD benchmarks, and ablation studies (e.g., on embedding choice). The computational cost of retraining CLIP models from scratch for each pruned dataset underscores the effort and thoroughness of the work.
3.  **Significant Findings:** The findings are highly significant for understanding the mechanisms behind the impressive generalization of large vision-language models like CLIP. By demonstrating that high train-test similarity is not the primary driver, the paper redirects attention to other data properties, fostering new research avenues. The release of a 100M core set of LAION is also a valuable practical contribution for the community.
4.  **Clarity:** The manuscript is generally well-written, logically structured, and easy to follow. The problem is clearly defined, the methodology is explained step-by-step, and the results are presented logically with clear figures and tables.

Weaknesses:
1.  **Limited Scope of Evaluation and Benchmarks:** This paper primarily focuses on common classification OOD benchmarks, neglecting a whole bunch of other crucial CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is a fundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is; the authors could consider using MSCOCO as an in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and Flickr-30k as an out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (as seen in recent datacomp papers), are strongly encouraged to broaden the applicability of the conclusions. The reliance on a single similarity metric, while explored with ablations on embedding choice, could also be further justified or expanded upon.
2.  **Missing or Ambiguous Experimental Details:** Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing or lack precision. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in Section 4.1 are constructed is not mentioned at all in the main text (or mentioned in the appendix without clear pointers). Specifically, the number of samples chosen in this set and the threshold of CLIP score used here are unclear. More critically, the definition of 'normalized similarity' in Appendix F is ambiguous and needs clarification to be technically sound.
3.  **Insufficient Comparative Analysis:** The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the *whole* LAION dataset (after filtering in Sections 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set might be similarly far from the test set, LAION still has a lot more samples that are *close* to the closest sample (though not as close as the single closest), potentially providing an unfair advantage on OOD benchmarks and thus compromising the validity of the conclusion.
4.  **Methodological and Assertional Precision Issues:** There are some precision issues in the methodology and claims. For instance, the assumption in Section 4.4 that ImageNet-Train is a 'subset' of LAION-400M is critical, but its explicit enforcement (adding ImageNet-Train to LAION-200M) is mentioned only later in Section 5. This represents a methodological sequencing flaw that impacts the transparency of the premise from the outset. Furthermore, the claim that 'dissimilar samples... act more like noise' (Section 4.2) is a strong interpretation requiring more rigorous justification or statistical analysis beyond just observing performance stability.
5.  **Presentation Inconsistencies:** The manuscript suffers from numerous minor inconsistencies in formatting, terminology, and presentation. Examples include inconsistent capitalization in section titles ('INTRODUCTION' vs. 'DISCUSSION'), varying abbreviations for 'Appendix' ('Appx.' vs. 'App.'), and the use of '-X' to denote reduction in dataset size in Table 1 rather than the resulting absolute size. The paper references color coding in Table 1's description ('Red color indicates a drop... blue represents a rise'), but the table itself does not contain colored text. The discussion of the 'effective robustness line' from Fang et al. in Section 6, while central to the argument, lacks sufficient context within this paper for a standalone assessment.

Questions:
1.  What is the potential broader impact or application of the findings in this paper? While this is a pure analytical work, and its merit is above the acceptance threshold, it would be valuable to learn what the authors envision as the broader implications of this work, such as how it might enlighten future research or engineering endeavors.
2.  The paper concludes that "other properties of the training data must drive CLIP to learn good representations." While scale and diversity are hinted at, could the authors elaborate on their hypotheses regarding which specific data properties (e.g., density of 'hard negatives', variety of contexts for objects, or the quality/compositionality of text captions) might be most impactful, based on their observations? Furthermore, how might one design rigorous experiments to quantitatively disentangle and measure the individual contributions of 'scale' versus 'diversity' in a similarly interventional manner?
3.  The 'combined-pruned' split shows a noticeable drop in performance across all six evaluation sets. Could the authors provide a more detailed analysis of the characteristics of the samples removed in this combined pruning strategy? Does this reveal any commonalities that might hint at other generalization drivers or relate to the concept of 'core' versus 'auxiliary' generalization factors?
4.  The definition of 'highly similar images' in Section 4.4 is relative to ImageNet-Train. Could the authors elaborate on how the choice of ImageNet-Train as the reference dataset (DS) impacts the interpretation of the results? For instance, if ImageNet-Train itself contains 'highly similar' examples to certain OOD benchmarks (e.g., specific styles or objects frequently co-occurring), how might this influence the 'similarity gap' and the conclusion that high train-test similarity is insufficient?
5.  In Section 5, when combining LAION-200M with ImageNet-Train, the authors state ImageNet-Train images are included with the caption "a photo of a {object class}". Could the authors elaborate on whether this standardized captioning might introduce a bias compared to the more diverse and potentially noisy captions found in LAION, and if this difference was considered in the analysis?
6.  The paper notes that 'far-pruning' can slightly surpass baseline performance (Sec 4.2). Could the authors provide more detailed statistical analysis on these instances? Is this improvement consistently significant, suggesting a genuine benefit from removing dissimilar 'noise', or within the margin of experimental variance?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.
reviews.strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"
reviews.questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper rigorously investigates whether the superior out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization performance of large foundation models like CLIP is primarily attributable to the presence of highly similar images in their vast training datasets, such as LAION. The authors propose a novel and systematic interventional methodology, using perceptual similarity to quantify train-test similarity and then pruning LAION to create subsets where this "similarity gap" to OOD benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R) matches that of ImageNet-Train.

The central finding is insightful: even after systematically pruning samples highly similar to OOD test sets, resulting in a considerable drop in the *absolute* OOD performance, CLIP models still show significant gains when compared to ImageNet-pretrained models under a fair train-test similarity comparison. This suggests that while dataset similarity plays a role, it is not the *key* or sole reason for CLIP's better OOD generalization. The work concludes that understanding the deeper reasons for CLIP's robust generalization remains an open question, likely pointing to other properties like dataset scale and diversity. The paper also introduces a 100M core set of LAION that matches original CLIP performance, a valuable practical contribution for the community.

**Strengths:**
1.  **Novel and Rigorous Methodology:** The paper introduces a highly original and systematic 'similarity gap' pruning methodology, which is a significant methodological contribution for isolating the effect of highly similar images in large datasets. This interventional approach provides a powerful tool for studying dataset properties.
2.  **Insightful and Computationally Intensive Experiments:** The work presents several insightful experimental results, involving extensive pruning strategies, diverse OOD benchmarks, and ablation studies. The computational cost of retraining CLIP models from scratch for each pruned dataset underscores the effort and thoroughness, providing valuable data for the community.
3.  **Significant Findings:** The core finding—that high train-test similarity is insufficient to fully explain CLIP's OOD generalization—is highly significant. It challenges a common intuition, redirecting attention to other data properties like dataset scale and diversity, fostering new research avenues into the mechanisms behind large model generalization.
4.  **Practical Contributions:** The release of the pruned datasets and the 100M core set of LAION is a valuable practical contribution, enabling further investigation and facilitating research for those with limited computational resources.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  **Insufficient Analysis for True OOD Testing:** While the experimental setup is comprehensive, a primary concern is that the analysis is not fully sufficient to enable truly fair **OOD** testing. OOD implies that the *distribution* or *domain* of images is unknown. The current pruning method removes samples that are *perceptually similar* to specific OOD test *samples*, but it does not guarantee the removal of all images from the entire *test domain* or *class* within the training set.
    *   For example, if there are five sketches of "airplane" in the OOD test set, only training images close to *these specific sketches* are removed. However, other "airplane sketches" that are farther away than the defined similarity threshold might remain.
    *   Furthermore, entire domains, such as "sketches" or "cartoon images," may still be broadly represented in the training data through other objects (e.g., "space shuttle" sketches not present in ImageNet but existing in LAION), thus making the *domain* itself not truly unseen by the model. Therefore, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" may still not be entirely meaningful even with the pruned datasets considered.
2.  **Technical Precision and Consistency Issues:** The paper, while generally well-structured, suffers from several areas lacking technical precision and consistency, which detracts from its overall quality.
    *   **Methodological Premise:** The assumption that ImageNet-Train is a 'subset' of LAION-400M (Sec 4.4) is critical, but its explicit enforcement (adding ImageNet-Train to LAION-200M) is mentioned only later (Sec 5), creating a methodological sequencing flaw that hinders transparency.
    *   **Strong Claims Lacking Rigor:** The claim that 'dissimilar samples... act more like noise' (Sec 4.2) is a strong interpretation that requires more rigorous justification or statistical analysis beyond just performance stability.
    *   **Ambiguous Definitions:** The definition of 'normalized similarity' in Appendix F is currently ambiguous and needs precise, per-sample formulation for technical soundness.
    *   **Formatting and Terminology Inconsistencies:** There are numerous minor inconsistencies throughout the manuscript, including varying capitalization in section titles, inconsistent abbreviations for 'Appendix', and unclear data presentation (e.g., using '-X' for dataset size reduction rather than absolute size in Table 1, and referencing non-existent color coding in Table 1's description).

**Minor Feedback and Questions:**
1.  **Clarity of Abstract and Contributions:** The clarity of the abstract and the contributions list could be improved by providing a shorter, more concise summary of the key findings.
2.  **Term Usage:** The term "generalization gap" is used in a different context in the literature; it would be better to give a different name to avoid confusion.
3.  **Typo:** Please correct the typo in "We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**" (should likely be ImageNet-Train or similar).
4.  **Further OOD Experiments:** To truly emulate an OOD setting, additional experiments are required. For instance, one should remove *all* "sketch" style images (or other relevant domains) from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) to test on ImageNet-sketch for a more rigorous OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.
5.  **Methodological Clarification (ImageNet-Train):** Please move the clarification regarding ImageNet-Train being explicitly added to LAION-200M from Section 5 to Section 4.4 to ensure the methodological premise is fully transparent from the outset.
6.  **Table 1 Presentation:** For Table 1, please present the 'Size' column for pruned datasets as the absolute number of samples (e.g., '198.6M') rather than a negative reduction, and either implement the described color coding or remove the descriptive text.
7.  **Context for 'Effective Robustness Line':** The discussion of the 'effective robustness line' from Fang et al. in Section 6, while central to the argument, lacks sufficient context within this paper for a standalone assessment. Please provide a brief explanation or conceptual plot.
8.  **Hypotheses on Other Properties:** While acknowledging this is future work, could the authors elaborate on their hypotheses regarding which specific data properties (e.g., semantic diversity, fine-grained details, specific visual styles, quality/compositionality of text captions) might be most impactful for generalization, based on their observations?
9.  **Analysis of Combined Pruning:** The 'combined-pruned' split shows a more substantial performance drop. A more detailed analysis of the characteristics of samples removed in this combined strategy, perhaps relating it to 'core' vs. 'auxiliary' generalization factors, would be beneficial.
10. **Bias from Standardized Captioning:** When combining LAION-200M with ImageNet-Train, the standardized captioning ("a photo of a {object class}") for ImageNet-Train images might introduce a bias compared to LAION's diverse and noisy captions. Was this difference considered in the analysis?
11. **Statistical Analysis for Far-Pruning:** For instances where 'far-pruning' slightly surpassed baseline performance (Sec 4.2), please provide a more detailed statistical analysis to confirm if this improvement is consistently significant or within experimental variance.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.
reviews.strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?
reviews.questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.

Strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

Weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?

Questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper investigates the hypothesis that CLIP's strong out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization performance, particularly on ImageNet-based benchmarks, primarily stems from a high similarity between its massive training dataset (LAION) and these test sets. The authors propose a novel methodology using perceptual similarity in CLIP's embedding space to quantify train-test similarity. They systematically prune LAION-200M to create subsets where the similarity gap to OOD benchmarks (like ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R) matches that of ImageNet-Train. Their central finding is that even after removing these 'highly similar images,' the retrained CLIP models exhibit only a marginal performance drop, still significantly outperforming ImageNet-trained models. This suggests that high train-test similarity alone is insufficient to explain CLIP's generalization, pointing instead to other properties like dataset scale and diversity. The work also introduces a 100M core set of LAION that matches original CLIP performance.

**Strengths:**

*   **Originality and Timeliness:** The paper addresses a crucial and timely question regarding the generalization of large foundation models, challenging a common intuition with a novel and systematic interventional approach. The concept of aligning the 'similarity gap' between datasets to isolate the effect of highly similar images is a significant methodological contribution.
*   **Clear Visual Evidence:** Figure 1 clearly demonstrates that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks, unlike the ImageNet training set. Figure 3 quantitatively reinforces this, showing that nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are, on average, more similar than those from the ImageNet-train set (except for ImageNet-Val). Figure 3.right also effectively illustrates the positive correlation between accuracy and having similar images in the training set for each OOD benchmark.
*   **Initial Evidence for Pruning Impact:** Figure 4 provides compelling initial evidence, showing that pruning LAION for samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in a substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. This indicates that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models on specific benchmarks.
*   **Technical Soundness and Scope:** The work is generally technically sound with extensive experiments, covering various pruning strategies, diverse OOD benchmarks, and ablation studies (e.g., on similarity metrics). The authors are careful in their analysis, clearly defining 'highly similar images' and acknowledging the limitations of their approach.
*   **Significance and Practical Contribution:** The findings are highly significant for understanding the mechanisms behind the impressive generalization of large vision-language models like CLIP. By demonstrating that high train-test similarity is not the *sole* or *primary* driver, the paper redirects attention to other data properties, fostering new research avenues. The release of a 100M core set of LAION is a valuable practical contribution for the community.

**Weaknesses:**

*   **Inconclusive Generalization Claims:** While Figure 4 shows the importance of near-pruned samples for *specific* OOD benchmarks, the results are not entirely conclusive regarding a holistic understanding of CLIP's OOD generalization.
    *   It is unclear whether a model *only* loses OOD generalization on one benchmark or if these near-pruned samples are crucial for broader image-classification performance. For instance, Figure 7 suggests that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val can result in lower performance across *other* ImageNet OOD datasets as well, implying these samples might contribute to a more general understanding of the ImageNet distribution. This necessitates plotting accuracy on multiple datasets (e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch) to show the specificity of the performance drop after targeted pruning.
    *   A comparison with classification models trained on ImageNet is missing. Pruning ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and comparing the accuracy drop with CLIP models would help determine if CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly or if any observed OOD generalization is solely due to seeing similar samples in the training set.
    *   The "combined-pruned" split shows a more noticeable and widespread drop in performance across all six evaluation sets compared to single-benchmark pruning. This suggests that while individual 'highly similar images' for one benchmark might not be critical, their collective removal across multiple benchmarks has a more pronounced effect. A deeper analysis of the characteristics of these collectively removed samples could provide further insights into other generalization drivers.
*   **Lack of Broader Discussion and Implications:** The paper is a prime opportunity for a broader discussion on "What is zero-shot?" and "What is out-of-distribution robustness?" After demonstrating that pruning similar samples loses OOD generalization, it remains unclear what the community should conclude regarding these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should CLIP models be considered as "cheating" the zero-shot definition? The objective of Sections 5 and 6, which aim to "Correct for highly similar images," is not fully elaborated in its implications. Why would one want to prune training datasets to achieve worse performance on test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? The rationale for training models on pruned datasets that perform worse (as seen in Table 1) needs further justification within a practical context. Furthermore, the discussion of the 'effective robustness line' from Fang et al. in Section 6, while central to the argument, lacks sufficient context for a standalone assessment within this paper.
*   **Methodological and Clarity Issues:**
    *   Some technical details lack precision. For example, the assumption that ImageNet-Train is a 'subset' of LAION-400M (Sec 4.4) is critical, but its explicit enforcement (adding ImageNet-Train to LAION-200M) is mentioned only later (Sec 5), which is a methodological sequencing flaw.
    *   The claim that 'dissimilar samples... act more like noise' (Sec 4.2) is a strong interpretation that requires more rigorous justification or statistical analysis beyond just performance stability. Similarly, the observation that 'near-pruning' on MNIST/SVHN (non-ImageNet-like datasets) sometimes *increases* performance implies that the benefit of 'highly similar images' might not be universal or that their definition needs refinement.
    *   The definition of 'normalized similarity' in Appendix F is ambiguous and needs clarification to be technically sound, as 'similarity gap' is defined as a set of values.
    *   While generally well-written, there are numerous minor inconsistencies in formatting, terminology, and presentation throughout the manuscript. Examples include inconsistent capitalization in section titles ('INTRODUCTION' vs. 'DISCUSSION'), varying abbreviations for 'Appendix' ('Appx.' vs. 'App.'), and the use of '-X' to denote reduction in dataset size in Table 1 rather than the resulting absolute size. The paper mentions color coding in Table 1's description ('Red color indicates a drop... blue represents a rise'), but the table itself does not contain colored text.

**Questions:**

*   Regarding the inconclusive generalization: Could the authors perform the two experiments outlined in the weaknesses section (i.e., plotting accuracy on two datasets after single-benchmark pruning, and comparing accuracy drops with ImageNet-trained models after similar pruning)?
*   What are the implications if the proposed method, and any model trained on such a pruned dataset, were considered transductive learning? Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that explicitly state, "ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model"?
*   Could the authors elaborate on their hypotheses regarding which specific data properties (e.g., density of 'hard negatives', variety of contexts for objects, semantic diversity, fine-grained details, specific visual styles not captured by ImageNet-Train's similarity profile, or the quality/compositionality of text captions) might be most impactful for generalization, beyond just scale and diversity? How might one design further rigorous experiments to quantitatively disentangle and measure the individual contributions of 'scale' versus 'diversity' in a similarly interventional manner?
*   Could the authors provide a more detailed analysis of the characteristics of the samples removed in the 'combined-pruned' strategy, given its more pronounced effect across benchmarks?
*   In Section 4.4, when ImageNet-Train is explicitly added to LAION-200M, it's mentioned that ImageNet-Train images are included with the caption "a photo of a {object class}". Could the authors elaborate on whether this standardized captioning might introduce a bias compared to the more diverse and potentially noisy captions found in LAION, and if this difference was considered in the analysis?
*   For the instances where 'far-pruning' can slightly surpass baseline performance (Sec 4.2), could the authors provide more detailed statistical analysis? Is this improvement consistently significant, suggesting a genuine benefit from removing dissimilar 'noise', or within the margin of experimental variance?
*   Could the authors clarify the choice of ImageNet-Train as the reference dataset (DS) for defining 'highly similar images' in Section 4.4 and how this choice might impact the interpretation of results?

**Typos and Minor Edits:**

*   Page 7: "Let us for consider" -> "Let us consider"
*   Ensure consistent capitalization for all section and subsection headers throughout the manuscript and appendices.
*   Implement the color coding in Table 1 as described in the text, or remove the descriptive text if colors cannot be used.
*   Present the 'Size' column for pruned datasets in Table 1 as the absolute number of samples (e.g., '198.6M') rather than a negative reduction, for clarity and consistency.

**Rating:** 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

Weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?

Questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Simple Hierarchical Planning with Diffusion

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.
reviews.strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.
reviews.weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.
reviews.questions: See weakness above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
